title: Factoring RSA Keys with Many Zeros
blurb: Researchers found RSA keys in the wild with suspiciously sparse moduli, regularly spaced blocks of zeros interleaved with random data, traced to certificates from Yahoo, Verizon, and NetApp devices, plus SSH hosts running EnterpriseDT's CompleteFTP. The bug appears across independent cryptographic implementations that failed in similar ways, suggesting cryptanalytic algorithms could be tailored to this failure mode and that other implementations may carry the same flaw.

title: GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna
blurb: OpenAI previewed GPT-5.6, a three-model family spanning Sol, its new flagship, Terra, a cheaper mid-tier option, and Luna, its fastest and most cost-efficient model. The release starts as a limited preview for trusted partners after coordination with the US government, and OpenAI classifies all three as High capability in cybersecurity and biological and chemical risk under its Preparedness Framework.

title: Anthropic says it is rolling back a covert Claude Code tracking feature to identify users based in China or affiliated with Chinese AI labs, after backlash (Juro Osawa/The Information)
blurb: Anthropic said it is rolling back a Claude Code feature that covertly flagged users based in China or affiliated with Chinese AI labs, following backlash over the tracking. The feature identified accounts by location and organizational ties, and its reversal highlights the tension between enforcing usage restrictions on frontier models and the privacy concerns those enforcement methods create.

title: French banking giant Crédit Agricole rolls out euro stablecoin, EURXT
blurb: Crédit Agricole, France's second-largest bank, launched EURXT, a euro-pegged, MiCA-compliant stablecoin issued on Ethereum by its Caceis asset-servicing arm and already used to settle a subscription into a tokenized Amundi fund. The token debuted with 20 million in circulation backed 1:1 by euro reserves, entering a market led by Circle's EURC and SocGen's EURCV and soon to face a 37-bank consortium called Qivalis.

title: BlueHammer Vulnerability Exploited in Ransomware Attacks
blurb: CISA confirmed that BlueHammer (CVE-2026-33825), a Microsoft Defender privilege-escalation flaw, is now being exploited in ransomware campaigns, updating its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities entry despite Microsoft still not acknowledging in-the-wild attacks. The bug was disclosed by a researcher feuding with Microsoft over its patch process, exploited as a zero-day by the time April fixes landed, and CISA does not notify defenders when a KEV entry gains ransomware activity.

title: You Don't Know Jack About Formal Verification
blurb: This ACM Queue article makes the case that formal verification has matured from academic exercise into practical tooling, using approachable examples to show how mathematically proving code correctness now fits into real engineering workflows. The author argues that most developers dismiss the technique based on outdated assumptions, and that modern tools like TLA+ and lightweight model checkers catch design bugs that tests never reach.

title: Understanding dynamic resource allocation in Kubernetes
blurb: Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) reached GA in Kubernetes v1.35, giving clusters a standard API for requesting and sharing devices like GPUs. NVIDIA has moved its dra-driver-nvidia-gpu into Kubernetes SIGs and dropped the Beta label, and this walkthrough shows how to allocate NVIDIA T10 and A5000 GPUs to workloads using the driver on a v1.35.3 cluster.

title: Sources: Meta is developing plans for a cloud infrastructure business that will sell access to AI computing power and models, to compete with AWS and Azure (Bloomberg)
blurb: Meta is developing plans for a cloud infrastructure business that would sell access to AI computing power and models, positioning it against AWS and Azure, according to Bloomberg. The move would turn Meta's massive internal GPU buildout into external revenue, marking its first serious entry into the cloud market long dominated by Amazon, Microsoft, and Google.

title: Apple Hide My Email bug seemingly allows 100% of real email addresses to be discovered
blurb: A security researcher disclosed a flaw in Apple's Hide My Email that let attackers reveal the real address behind every generated alias, with tests reportedly succeeding on 100% of addresses. EasyOptOuts co-founder Tyler Murphy said he reported the bug to Apple over a year ago and went public after repeated missed fix deadlines, and 404 Media confirmed the vulnerability remains exploitable.

title: U.S. lifting export control restrictions on Anthropic’s Mythos, Fable
blurb: The U.S. Commerce Department lifted export controls on Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models after weeks of negotiation, restoring public and foreign access alongside new guardrails and classifiers meant to block jailbreaks. The controls followed an Amazon report on jailbreaking Fable's cybersecurity capabilities, though Anthropic later showed that existing models like GPT-5.5, Claude Opus 4.8, and Kimi K2.7 could identify the same vulnerabilities.

title: Anthropic restores Claude Fable 5 as US lifts export controls — single filter now blocks prompt that could identify software vulnerabilities and write code to exploit them
blurb: Anthropic restored global access to Claude Fable 5 a day after the US Department of Commerce withdrew the export controls it imposed on June 12th, ending an 18-day standoff. The fix was a single classifier that blocks the Amazon-flagged jailbreak in over 99% of cases and reroutes those requests to Opus 4.8, after government testing confirmed that GPT-5.5, Kimi K2.7, and weaker models could find the same vulnerabilities.

title: Abu Dhabi-based MGX raised a $49B AI-focused fund, exceeding its $45B target, and plans to spend as much as $10B annually over the next few years (Dinesh Nair/Bloomberg)
blurb: MGX, an Abu Dhabi-backed investment firm, raised a $49B AI-focused fund, exceeding its $45B target and ranking among the largest vehicles dedicated to the sector. The firm plans to deploy as much as $10B annually over the next few years, adding another deep-pocketed sovereign backer to a market already crowded with capital chasing AI infrastructure and model developers.

title: Asahi Linux 7.1 Progress Report
blurb: Asahi Linux shipped its 7.1 release with a fix for a boot-picker regression that macOS 27's Golden Gate beta introduced, which had made Linux installs vanish from Apple's Startup Disk and boot picker. The upgrade began ignoring an APFS "bootable" metadata flag Asahi never set, so new installs now set it automatically and an installer repair mode restores existing systems.

title: NSA's SIGINT Enabling Project Pushes Weakened TLS Post-Quantum Standard
blurb: The author alleges that the NSA's SIGINT Enabling Project is paying to standardize "ietf-tls-mlkem," a weaker post-quantum TLS key exchange than the hybrid "ietf-tls-ecdhe-mlkem," and is now packing an IETF TLS working-group vote with first-time participants after losing an earlier round. The post urges readers to join the mailing list and register opposition to draft-ietf-tls-mlkem-08 before the working-group last call ends on 8 July 2026.

title: Amazon EKS now supports control plane egress through your VPC
blurb: Amazon EKS now lets you route Kubernetes control plane egress through your own VPC, applying your security groups and firewall rules.

title: Meta pops 8% as company makes cloud push to sell excess AI compute power capacity
blurb: Meta is building a cloud business to sell its excess AI compute to outside customers, sending shares up 8% on Wednesday. The move could help recoup part of the up to $145B in capex Meta plans to spend this year, but it pushes the company into a market dominated by Amazon, Microsoft, Google, and CoreWeave.

title: Together AI, which offers access to open-source models, raised $800M led by Saudi Aramco's Prosperity7 at an $8.3B valuation, taking its total funding to $1.3B (Niko Gallogly/New York Times)
blurb: Together AI raised $800M at an $8.3B valuation in a round led by Saudi Aramco's Prosperity7, bringing its total funding to $1.3B. The company provides cloud infrastructure for running and fine-tuning open-source models, positioning it against hosted-model providers as demand for open-weight alternatives grows.

title: Taiwanese authorities detain two Super Micro staff and an Albatron manager after a raid of Super Micro's local offices this week over Nvidia shipments to China (Bloomberg)
blurb: Taiwanese authorities detained two Super Micro employees and an Albatron manager after raiding Super Micro's local offices this week, part of a probe into Nvidia chip shipments to China. The arrests mark an escalation in Taiwan's enforcement of export controls, putting hardware makers and their supply-chain partners under scrutiny as US restrictions on advanced AI chips reaching China tighten.

title: Qwen Image Agent
blurb: Alibaba's Qwen team introduced Qwen-Image-Agent, an agentic framework that wraps a text-to-image model in planning, reasoning, search, and memory to fill in underspecified or knowledge-dependent prompts. The system treats user input as partial context and builds out the full generation context before rendering, reaching state-of-the-art results on the new IA-Bench, Mindbench, and WISE-Verified benchmarks against strong baselines.

title: Apple Patches Dozens of Vulnerabilities Across iOS, macOS, and Safari
blurb: Apple released iOS 26.5.2, iPadOS 26.5.2, macOS Tahoe 26.5.2, and Safari 26.5.2, patching dozens of vulnerabilities, including 26 in WebKit that malicious websites could exploit to leak data, corrupt memory, or escape the sandbox. At least four of the defects were surfaced with AI and reported by researchers at Anthropic and OpenAI, signaling a growing role for automated tooling in vulnerability discovery.

title: Letter: the US says Anthropic "agreed to proactively detect and address security risks" of Fable 5 and Mythos 5; a source says Anthropic developed a "safeguard" (Financial Times)
blurb: Anthropic agreed to proactively detect and address security risks in its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models, according to a US government letter cited by the Financial Times. A source familiar with the matter said Anthropic has already developed a specific safeguard, suggesting the commitment reflects concrete technical controls rather than a general pledge on model safety.

title: Taiwan Detains Super Micro Workers in China Chip Smuggling Probe
blurb: Taiwanese prosecutors detained two Super Micro employees and raided the company's local offices over alleged shipments of Nvidia chips to China. The probe signals tightening enforcement of US export controls at the supply-chain level, where Taiwan-based intermediaries route restricted AI hardware into China despite the ban.

title: NIH unveils the world’s largest genomics-and-health database
blurb: The NIH's All of Us program released data from more than 747,000 participants, creating the world's largest integrated store of genomes and electronic health records by linking 535,000 whole genome sequences to nearly 482,000 medical records. More than 86% of participants come from groups underrepresented in medical research, giving AI drug-discovery and risk-prediction tools the kind of diverse, genomic-plus-clinical data that repositories like the UK Biobank lack.

title: Microsoft Accelerates Post-Quantum Cryptography Shift to 2029
blurb: Microsoft moved up its Quantum Safe Program timeline, aiming to transition critical products and services to post-quantum cryptography by 2029 as it warns that cryptographically relevant quantum computers could arrive sooner than expected. The plan prioritizes TLS 1.3 adoption, crypto-agility for stored data, and securing trust chains like code signing and certificate issuance, and follows a Trump executive order setting PQC deadlines for federal agencies.

title: Gemini Spark, Google’s agentic assistant, is now available on Mac
blurb: Google brought Gemini Spark, its agentic assistant, to macOS in beta for Google AI Ultra subscribers in the US, letting it work with local files and connect to apps like Google Tasks, Keep, Canva, Dropbox, and Instacart. The launch adds real-time topic tracking and custom MCP support, positioning Spark against desktop rivals like Claude Desktop, Microsoft Copilot, and OpenClaw.

title: US Limits on Anthropic’s Mythos Keep Foreign Firms in Limbo
blurb: The Trump administration continues to restrict access to Anthropic's Mythos 5 to a limited number of US organizations, even after dropping export controls on the company's latest AI models. Foreign governments, companies, and financial institutions remain in limbo, unable to secure access as Washington keeps the most capable tier reserved for domestic use.

title: The Orbital Data Center Hype Machine Is Already in Orbit
blurb: A new IEEE Spectrum cover story argues that orbital data centers remain far from viable, despite Elon Musk's claim that space will be the cheapest place to run AI within three years and SpaceX's FCC filing for a 1-million-satellite constellation. The math is the obstacle: deploying that many satellites would demand roughly 16,666 dedicated Starship launches and, at a tenfold manufacturing increase, about 25 years to build.

title: OpenAI, Anthropic backer MGX raises one of the biggest AI funds ever as it closes at $49 billion
blurb: Abu Dhabi's MGX closed a $49B fund to back AI companies, one of the largest vehicles ever focused on the sector and above its initial $45B target. The Emirati fund is already a major backer of Anthropic and OpenAI, having co-led Anthropic's $30B round and OpenAI's $122B raise, and now plans to invest across semiconductors, infrastructure, and AI platforms.

title: Anthropic Economic Index June 2026 Report
blurb: Anthropic's June 2026 Economic Index report finds that Claude usage now mirrors the rhythms of daily life, with work queries dropping on weekends, news requests peaking in the morning, and tax questions surging near filing deadlines. The report also shows that more compute correlates with more valuable outputs, and previews the first results from the Anthropic Economic Index Survey linking self-reported expectations to actual usage data.

title: A peek into Reddit's anti-spam internals
blurb: A 2021 Reddit glitch briefly exposed the site's automated spam-removal internals, replacing the generic "Removed: Auto" label with the raw signals behind each decision. The captured screenshots reveal a per-comment scoring system that weighs account age, karma, report counts, a Perspective API spam probability, email domain, TLS fingerprints, and network organization to flag likely spam.

title: Figma Grows 46% and Trades at 6x. Everyone Else Growing 30%+ Trades at 15-36x.
blurb: Figma trades near 6x forward revenue despite growing 46% with two straight quarters of acceleration, 82% gross margins, 139% net dollar retention, and a 27% free cash flow margin, while software peers growing 30% or more trade at 15-36x. The author argues the stock's 87% fall from its post-IPO peak reflects a fake pop, sector-wide AI repricing, and a bet that tools like Claude Design erode Figma's moat.

title: Google Told to Pay Klarna Nearly $2 Billion in Shopping Spat
blurb: A Swedish court ordered Google to pay nearly $2 billion to Klarna's Pricerunner unit, finding that Google abused its market power by favoring its own comparison shopping service over rivals. The ruling adds to Google's growing antitrust liabilities in Europe, where regulators and competitors have repeatedly challenged how the company ranks shopping results in search.

title: What can you confidently guarantee about your software?
blurb: The author argues that most teams can guarantee far less about their software than they assume, because testing, types, and code review each cover only narrow slices of actual behavior. The piece walks through where common assurance techniques break down and makes the case for being explicit about which properties you can actually prove versus the ones you merely hope hold.

title: Base's Two-Hour Outage Exposes Single-Sequencer Risk
blurb: Base went down for nearly two hours after its single sequencer processed one invalid block, halting all blocks until operators manually restarted nodes.

title: Devin Fusion
blurb: Cognition released Devin Fusion, a multi-model harness that maintains frontier and Fable 5-level performance at 35% lower cost on FrontierCode, its new coding benchmark measuring both correctness and quality. The system runs a frontier agent alongside a cheaper "sidekick" model, delegating routine work and routing dynamically mid-session while the main agent handles planning, ambiguity, and final review. It is available in preview at app.devin.ai.

title: A researcher says a vulnerability in Apple's Hide My Email tool lets anyone discover a real email address; first reported in June 2025, Apple is yet to fix it (Joseph Cox/404 Media)
blurb: A researcher disclosed a vulnerability in Apple's Hide My Email that lets anyone unmask the real address behind a relay alias, according to 404 Media. The flaw was first reported to Apple in June 2025 and remains unpatched a year later, undercutting a privacy feature that iCloud+ subscribers rely on to shield their identities.

title: Sony to end physical PlayStation game disc sales in 2028
blurb: Sony will stop producing physical discs for all new PlayStation games starting January 2028, shifting new releases to digital-only sales through the PlayStation Store and other retailers. The move follows digital downloads reaching 85% of full-game sales on PS4 and PS5, with games released before the cutoff still available on disc.

title: Cambrige experts find utterly simple fix for longer lasting EV batteries. Just put some pressure on it.
blurb: University of Cambridge researchers found that keeping lithium-ion cells under steady mechanical pressure can roughly double their lifespan without altering the chemistry, electrolyte, or active materials. The Nature Energy study identified a sweet spot near 12.5 bar, where too little pressure accelerated cathode cracking and too much triggered lithium plating, though the pressure-control system remains lab-scale and unproven at production volume.

title: ByteDance Picks Brazil for Its Largest Data Center Outside China
blurb: ByteDance chose Brazil for its largest data center outside China, a $39B facility that marks the latest win from Lula's push to attract Chinese investment. The buildout deepens ByteDance's infrastructure footprint in Latin America and gives the TikTok owner regional compute capacity as it scales AI and recommendation workloads beyond its home market.

title: Sony is killing all physical PlayStation game discs
blurb: Sony will stop producing physical discs for new PlayStation games starting January 2028, moving all new releases to digital-only distribution through the PlayStation Store and other retailers. Games released before that date will still be available on disc, but the shift ends resale value and game-sharing for future titles and ties access to Sony's servers and account system.

title: Stockholm's Patent and Market Court orders Google to pay nearly $2B to Klarna's PriceRunner in a dispute over abuse of power in the shopping comparison market (Bloomberg)
blurb: A Stockholm court ordered Google to pay nearly $2B to Klarna's PriceRunner, ruling that Google abused its market power in shopping comparison services. The judgment from the Patent and Market Court is one of the largest private antitrust awards against Google in Europe, and it adds to the company's mounting legal losses over how it ranks its own shopping results.

title: Single Dose of Frog-Derived Gut Bacterium Eradicates 100% of Tumors in Mice
blurb: A single intravenous dose of Ewingella americana, a bacterium isolated from amphibian and reptile gut microbiomes, eradicated tumors in 100% of mice with colorectal cancer and no detectable toxicity, according to a new study in Gut Microbes. The naturally occurring microbe homes to the low-oxygen tumor environment, multiplies roughly 3,000-fold within 24 hours, and triggers a broad immune response, outperforming both chemotherapy and anti-PD-L1 immunotherapy in the preclinical model.

title: Wayve files to sell shares on the London Stock Exchange's new Private Securities Market, the first major company to test it, and let staff sell $85M in shares (Bloomberg)
blurb: Wayve filed to list shares on the London Stock Exchange's new Private Securities Market, becoming the first major company to test the venue and letting staff sell $85M in stock. The move gives the UK self-driving startup a liquidity path without a full IPO, while serving as an early proof point for the LSE's effort to keep high-growth private firms trading in London.

title: Exclusive: Lunar Forge Unveils Plans to Build Lunar Infrastructure Out of Dust
blurb: Lunar Forge emerged from stealth with a plan to laser-sinter lunar regolith into construction material, targeting housing and shielding for the Moon's fission reactors. The startup says building in-situ sidesteps the roughly $1M/kg cost of shipping materials from Earth, and its lab samples hit compressive strengths of 200 to 345 MPa, well above concrete's 20 to 30 MPa.

title: Google might be testing Gemini Flash upgrade on LM Arena
blurb: An unannounced Gemini Flash checkpoint on LM Arena appears a step above the app's current version, and Google's Arena listings have reliably preceded launches.

title: Figma's design agent, now with custom tools and greater context
blurb: Figma expanded its canvas-based design agent with custom tools, skills, and richer context about how a team works. Designers can now prompt the agent to build reusable generative plugins using PropsKit so they feel native to Figma, plus create custom shader effects and fills, without needing a developer or a separate dev setup.

title: Tesla hires 17-year Intel veteran responsible for billion-dollar fab startups — Gary Jiang likely chosen to oversee fab efforts for Terafab's licensing of 14A
blurb: Tesla hired Gary Jiang, a 17-year Intel veteran who most recently transferred Intel's 18A process from its Oregon development fab to high-volume production in Arizona, as the first publicly identified senior leader for Musk's Terafab chip project. The joint effort between Tesla, SpaceX, and xAI still lacks a formal head, but Jiang brings hands-on experience running billion-dollar fab startups as it reportedly licenses Intel's 14A node.

title: Godot will no longer accept AI-authored code contributions
blurb: The Godot Foundation will bar AI-authored code, AI-submitted pull requests, and AI-generated text from human communication, after months of maintainers being overwhelmed by low-effort "slop" contributions. The project argues that AI cannot take responsibility for its output and that heavy AI users often don't understand their own code well enough to fix it, so new policies will add barriers to low-effort PRs and prioritize mentoring accountable human contributors.

title: AI researchers trick chatbots into sharing how to make cocaine as long as they believe a user is wearing a green shirt — 'CoT Forgery' exploit spurs LLMs to divulge forbidden info by faking trusted chains of thought
blurb: A new paper called "Prompt Injection as Role Confusion" shows that LLMs judge a prompt's authority by its writing style rather than the role tags meant to separate trusted commands from untrusted data, letting attackers forge fake chains of thought the model treats as its own reasoning. The technique, dubbed CoT Forgery, lifted jailbreak success from near zero to roughly 60% across every model tested and won a 2025 OpenAI red-teaming contest.

title: Blue Origin accelerates LC-36 recovery via hybrid plan
blurb: Blue Origin is racing to repair Launch Complex-36 at Cape Canaveral after last month's New Glenn anomaly, with CEO Dave Limp still targeting a return to flight before the end of 2026 despite forecasts of a 12- to 18-month stand-down. Early analysis points to the booster's aft section as the anomaly's origin, and the company is adopting a new hybrid concept of operations for future launches.

title: Meta, like SpaceX, looks to turn excess AI compute into cash
blurb: Meta is developing a cloud infrastructure business to sell access to its AI compute and models, pitting it against AWS, Google Cloud, and Microsoft Azure, according to Bloomberg. The move mirrors xAI's recent compute leases and aims to monetize part of Meta's $182.9B AI infrastructure commitment, including a Manhattan-sized Ohio data center that has yet to yield a material standalone revenue line.

title: Working With AI: A concrete example
blurb: Carson Gross, creator of htmx and hyperscript, walks through a real debugging session where Claude correctly found the root cause of a parser regression but proposed a fix that papered over the problem rather than addressing it. He uses the example to illustrate the "Sorcerer's Apprentice" risk: leaning on AI for solutions you don't fully understand can leave you unable to maintain your own systems.

title: US Government allows Anthropic limited release of AI model that sparked cybersecurity concerns
blurb: The US government cleared Anthropic to redeploy its Mythos 5 cybersecurity model to a small group of vetted cyber defenders and infrastructure providers, reversing an export block imposed earlier in June over national security concerns. The revised license excludes Fable 5, the weaker variant still awaiting general-release approval, and underscores the absence of a consistent US regulatory framework for frontier AI.

title: The inside scoop on alerting changes in Kubernetes Monitoring
blurb: Grafana Cloud is consolidating Kubernetes Monitoring alerts onto Grafana-managed alerting, so reinstalls may silently break notifications routed through the old Mimir Alertmanager.

title: Bending Spoons IPO Turns Italian College Pals Into Billionaires
blurb: Bending Spoons went public, turning its four college-friend founders into Italy's youngest self-made billionaires. The Milan-based software rollup, known for buying and reviving apps like Evernote, WeTransfer, and Meetup, has grown into one of Europe's most aggressive acquirers of consumer software.

title: Blue Origin outlines new launch pad approach as it pushes to return New Glenn to flight
blurb: Blue Origin plans to return New Glenn to flight by the end of 2026 using a new horizontal/vertical hybrid launch process instead of rebuilding the transporter/erector destroyed in the May 28 pad explosion. The rocket will roll out horizontally, be raised by crane, and have its payload attached on the pad, a recovery that would take roughly seven months while the cause, traced to the first stage's aft section, remains under investigation.

title: Sony kills physical PlayStation games. The era of discs comes to an end for Team Blue
blurb: Sony will stop producing physical discs for new PlayStation games starting January 2028, making all titles released after that date digital-only through the PlayStation Store or retailers. Existing discs and current consoles keep working, but the move formalizes a shift Sony had already signaled with the all-digital PS5 in 2020, the disc-free PS5 Slim in 2023, and the driveless PS5 Pro in 2025.

title: DRAM it! Cheap PCs being priced out of existence as memory cost bites
blurb: Surging DRAM prices are pushing entry-level PCs toward extinction as memory costs consume a growing share of each build's bill of materials. With AI datacenter demand soaking up supply and manufacturers prioritizing high-margin server chips, budget machines face price hikes that erode the low end of the market.

title: Virginia county asks all employees, including schools, to conserve power due to AI-driven electricity price hikes — state's 400-plus data centers steadily increasing demand, grid expansion, and pricing
blurb: Henrico County, Virginia asked all employees, including those in schools and social services, to conserve power as data-center demand drives repeated electricity rate hikes. Government entities in the state face a 24.9% increase next month, part of a broader squeeze from Northern Virginia's 400-plus data centers, which push utilities like Dominion Energy into costly grid buildouts that critics say land on households.

title: DeepSeek breaks China’s AI price war with peak-hour surge pricing
blurb: DeepSeek told API customers it will double the price of its V4 models during peak hours, its first time charging by the clock. Output tokens for deepseek-v4-pro rise from roughly $0.85 to $1.70 per million during two daily windows in Beijing time, a surge-pricing move that reverses the aggressive undercutting that made DeepSeek the trigger of China's AI price war.

title: The Next Paradigm
blurb: The author argues that the labs' current bet, training AIs on millions of verifiable tasks across thousands of RL environments, may reach AGI by treating the paradigm's apparent limits as engineering problems rather than fundamental ones. Sample inefficiency is a one-time training cost amortized across billions of sessions, and continual learning could become unnecessary if in-context learning keeps extending to longer horizons, making weight updates from deployment optional.

title: The "Akrites" vulnerability-mitigation project launches
blurb: Akrites is a new project that fixes vulnerabilities upstream and keeps them confidential until patches are widely deployed, citing AI-accelerated exploit development.

title: Free The Icons
blurb: Apple's macOS Tahoe forces every app icon into a uniform squircle; this post argues for restoring distinct icon shapes for usability and personality.

title: Twelve Labs, which is building AI models to make video searchable and understandable, raised a $100M Series B co-led by NEA and Naver, and signs an AWS deal (Saritha Rai/Bloomberg)
blurb: Twelve Labs raised a $100M Series B co-led by NEA and Naver to expand its models for making video searchable and understandable. The round accompanies a new AWS deal that will distribute Twelve Labs' technology to cloud customers, deepening competition to turn raw video into queryable data.

title: Video Search Startup Raises $100 Million From Amazon, VC Funds
blurb: Twelve Labs raised $100M from investors including Amazon, NEA, and Naver Ventures to build AI that searches and analyzes large video libraries. The round backs a startup focused on video understanding, a capability that multimodal models still handle poorly, and gives Amazon a strategic stake as it expands its own AI infrastructure.

title: AI video startup Higgsfield is in talks to raise at a $5bn valuation
blurb: Higgsfield, the AI video startup founded in 2023, is in talks to raise $300M to $500M at a $5B pre-money valuation, quadruple its $1.3B mark in January. The round, with DST Global among the investors, follows the company crossing a $500M annualized revenue run rate this month, up from $200M at the end of 2025, with roughly 70% coming from enterprise customers.

title: Google's New reCAPTCHA Wants Your Camera Access and 21 Points of Your Hand
blurb: Google is testing a new reCAPTCHA challenge that asks users to enable their camera and wave, extracting 21 hand-landmark coordinates to verify they are human rather than a bot. Google frames the check as liveness detection and says footage is deleted after verification, never tied to identity, and not shared with third parties, though it remains an optional biometric scan governed by the company's broad privacy policy.

title: Oracle Warns Its Splurge on AI Data Centers May Not Pay Off
blurb: Oracle warned that its aggressive spending on AI data centers may not generate adequate returns, flagging the risk in a company disclosure about its cloud build-out. The caution stands out because Oracle has staked its cloud growth on multibillion-dollar capacity commitments to meet AI demand, and it signals that even committed hyperscalers are unsure whether current AI infrastructure spending will pay off.

title: Google built a great smart speaker, but Gemini isn’t ready for it
blurb: Google released the Google Home Speaker, a $99.99 device that is its first new smart speaker in six years and the first built for Gemini for Home. The Verge's review praises the hardware and Gemini's conversational understanding but calls the assistant slow, unreliable, and partly paywalled, concluding that the software is not yet ready for the hardware it ships on.

title: Adobe is Buying Topaz Labs, the AI Video Enhancer
blurb: Adobe agreed to acquire Topaz Labs, the Emmy-winning maker of AI tools that upscale, sharpen, denoise, and restore images and video. The terms were undisclosed, and Adobe plans to fold Topaz's models into Firefly and Creative Cloud apps like Photoshop, Lightroom, and Premiere, gaining on-device technology that runs heavy AI video processing locally instead of in the cloud.

title: Build from anywhere with Cursor for iOS
blurb: Cursor released a native iOS app in public beta that lets developers launch and control coding agents from their phones. Users can start cloud-based agents or remote-control agents running on their computer, pick any frontier model, guide work with voice input and slash commands, and review PRs, screenshots, and logs, with lock-screen Live Activities and push notifications tracking progress.

title: Salesforce employees are confused about why the company is promoting a competitor inside Slack
blurb: Anthropic launched Claude Tag, an always-on AI agent that works inside Slack channels, and Salesforce promoted it publicly even though it competes directly with Salesforce's own Slackbot and Agentforce. The overlap is awkward but financially logical: Salesforce, which owns Slack, expects to spend $300M on Anthropic tokens this year and holds a roughly 1% stake in Anthropic, now valued at $380B.

title: A UN panel co-chaired by Yoshua Bengio warns that AI capabilities are outpacing scientific understanding, the "potential benefits of AI are enormous", and more (Andrea Shalal/Reuters)
blurb: A UN scientific panel co-chaired by Yoshua Bengio warned that AI capabilities are advancing faster than researchers' ability to understand or govern them. The report calls the potential benefits enormous while flagging risks from labor displacement to misuse, and urges coordinated international oversight before the gap between deployment and comprehension widens further.

title: Supreme Court ruling guts government's use of geofence warrants
blurb: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that the Fourth Amendment protects a phone's "location history," requiring police to obtain a warrant with probable cause before using geofence warrants that pull every device logged in a given area. Writing for the majority, Justice Kagan found the amount of data seized irrelevant, since users routinely opt into location tracking to make apps work and Google rarely discloses how that data can reach the government.

title: NASA Praises Blue Origin’s Mishap Progress, Announces Moon Base Contracts
blurb: NASA awarded roughly $600M in new lunar delivery contracts to Astrobotic, Intuitive Machines, and Firefly, and said Blue Origin is recovering well from last month's New Glenn explosion. The vendors will fly updated versions of already-flown lander designs to support NASA's goal of monthly Moon landings, while Blue Origin remains a candidate to fly Artemis III no earlier than 2027.

title: Wayve undertakes $85M employee tender offer to “retain talent”
blurb: Wayve launched an $85M employee tender offer, letting staff sell equity to new and existing investors as the UK autonomous-driving startup works to retain talent. The move follows its $8.6B valuation from February's $1.2B Series D and marks Wayve's second tender offer, joining Stripe, ElevenLabs, and Revolut in using the mechanism as IPO markets stay frozen.

title: How Lime went from pandemic write-off to a $167M IPO
blurb: Lime priced its Nasdaq IPO at $25 a share on June 30, raising $167 million and returning the micromobility company to public markets after its valuation crashed from $2.4 billion in 2019 to about $510 million in 2020. Uber, whose 2020 investment and in-app integration revived Lime's demand, plans to buy up to $20 million of shares in the offering.

title: Range anxiety
blurb: Rivian is betting its future on the R2, a more affordable midsize SUV meant to broaden its reach beyond luxury-priced EVs, even as US demand cools and legacy automakers retreat from electrification. A tornado tore through the roof of the company's Normal, Illinois factory in April, striking the exact section where R2 production was set to begin within days.

title: Tech and Finance Sectors Losing 28,000 Jobs Monthly Show AI Impact on Labor
blurb: US tech and finance sectors are shedding roughly 28,000 jobs a month, the clearest sign yet that AI is showing up in employment data rather than just forecasts. Whether the technology drives sustained mass cuts is still contested, but the trend marks a shift from speculation about AI's labor impact to measurable losses in the sectors closest to the technology.

title: Sony says all new PlayStation games from both first- and third-party developers will be sold in digital formats from January 2028, ending physical game discs (Stephen Totilo/Game File)
blurb: Sony will require all new PlayStation games from both first- and third-party developers to sell in digital-only formats starting January 2028, ending physical game discs. The shift removes disc-based ownership and resale for new releases, pushing the platform fully toward digital distribution and tightening Sony's control over sales and pricing.

title: Android 17 makes it harder for bad actors to guess and crack the PIN on your phone
blurb: Android 17 caps unlock attempts far more aggressively, cutting five-year PIN guesses from 1,800 to 19 and locking the phone after 20 failures.

title: OpenAI’s first hardware is a macro pad for Codex coders
blurb: OpenAI teased Codex Micro, its first hardware product, a macro pad for AI coding built with keyboard maker Work Louder and launching July 15. The programmable key pad, closely resembling Work Louder's Creator Micro 2, likely maps physical buttons to repetitive Codex actions like starting agents, running tests, and approving changes, and is separate from the consumer device OpenAI is developing with Jony Ive.

title: EchoStar’s satellite TV and wireless subsidiaries file for bankruptcy
blurb: EchoStar subsidiaries tied to its satellite TV and abandoned 5G businesses, including DISH DBS and DISH Wireless, filed for Chapter 11 in Texas to advance a prepackaged restructuring after selling more than $40B in spectrum to SpaceX and AT&T. The filing follows delayed AT&T sale proceeds that left DISH DBS unable to cover $2B in debt due July 1, and it leaves DISH TV, Sling TV, and Boost Mobile operations untouched.

title: Getty Images is canceling its $3.7 billion Shutterstock merger due to UK restrictions
blurb: Getty Images terminated its $3.7B merger with Shutterstock after the UK's Competition and Markets Authority demanded Shutterstock sell its global editorial business, including its celebrity and news photo agencies. The deal had already cleared the US DoJ unconditionally, showing that American approval alone can't close a cross-border merger and foreshadowing the risk Paramount faces on its Warner Bros. Discovery deal.

title: Reward Models Can Be Too Sensitive
blurb: This paper shows many reward models are oversensitive, scoring equally good responses differently, and proposes Monte Carlo dropout discretization to cut reward hacking.

title: FokosDB: A strongly consistent bottomless storage database ontop of Cloudflare Durable Objects
blurb: A developer built FokosDB, a strongly consistent, bottomless-storage database on top of Cloudflare Durable Objects, and details its architecture and trade-offs.

title: New FBI Alert: Russian Intelligence Uses Signal Recovery Keys to Access Messages
blurb: The FBI and CISA updated their March 2026 advisory to warn that FSB-linked groups, now tracked as UNC5792 and UNC4221, have shifted from phishing Signal verification codes to stealing users' Backup Recovery Keys. Unlike a one-time code, a recovery key unlocks the entire message archive and keeps working even after the victim creates a new account, giving attackers persistent access to historical private and group messages.

title: PlayStation ditching disc games entirely, calling it a "natural direction" for Sony
blurb: Sony announced it will discontinue physical disc production for all new PlayStation games starting January 2028, moving to a fully digital release model. Games already out or launching before that date keep their discs, and retailers will still sell boxed copies containing download codes, signaling that the next PlayStation console will likely be digital-only.

title: Voltage Glitching Attack Bypasses Safety Checks in PX4 Autopilot Flight Controllers
blurb: Security researchers at UC demonstrated a voltage glitching attack that bypasses safety checks on the PX4 Autopilot flight controller, letting them skip or inject control instructions on STMicroelectronics STM32 chips widely used in research, hobbyist, and commercial UAVs. The fault injection can alter failsafe mechanisms and disrupt instruction execution, though it currently requires physical access to the target device.

title: Data Breach at Indian Supplier Tata Electronics Exposes iPhone 18 Pro Details and Photos
blurb: A ransomware group breached Apple supplier Tata Electronics and posted stolen files to the dark web, including component lists and photos of the unreleased iPhone 18 Pro. The leaked documents map hundreds of parts to specific suppliers, data Apple keeps off its public supplier list, exposing its sourcing leverage and single-supplier vulnerabilities to rivals and counterfeiters.

title: Microsoft reportedly plans to lay off under 2.5% of its workforce to reduce costs
blurb: Microsoft plans to cut fewer than 2.5% of its 220,000-person workforce, roughly 5,500 employees, in a round expected to be announced next week. The cuts hit sales, consulting, and Xbox gaming, and follow a May 2024 reduction of 3% and April buyouts accepted by about a third of eligible US staff, as the company redirects spending toward AI infrastructure.

title: Chamath Palihapitiya just took his first full-time operating job since Facebook — and the $135M cap table behind it reveals what the AI coding war is really about
blurb: Chamath Palihapitiya took the CEO seat at 8090 Labs, his first full-time operating role since leaving Facebook, after the enterprise AI coding startup raised a $135M Series A led by Salesforce Ventures. 8090's Software Factory targets corporate teams that need production-grade, auditable code, putting it up against Anthropic's Claude Code and OpenAI's tooling for Fortune 500 procurement budgets.

title: Oxmiq, which aims to combine GPUs, CPUs, and a tensor engine into a single block of IP that it can license, raised $35M led by Samsung Catalyst Fund and Fudomo (Max A. Cherney/Reuters)
blurb: Oxmiq raised $35M led by Samsung Catalyst Fund and Fudomo to license a single IP block combining GPUs, CPUs, and a tensor engine.

title: How the rapid rise in US- and China-made AI abilities is leading to both a transformation in AI use at work, and sudden lurches in policies and markets (Ethan Mollick/One Useful Thing)
blurb: Ethan Mollick argues that fast-improving AI models from US and Chinese labs are quietly reshaping how people actually work, even as the surrounding policy and market landscape swings unpredictably. The essay contends that capability gains are outpacing institutions' ability to adapt, producing a gap between what AI can do at work and how regulators and markets respond.

title: Musk Says Grok 4.5 Entered Private Beta
blurb: Musk says Grok 4.5, built on the 1.5T V9 model, is in private beta at SpaceX and Tesla with near-Opus evals.

title: Google Cloud will sell specialist AI models built for science
blurb: Google Cloud will begin selling SandboxAQ's specialist "large quantitative models" through its marketplace, offering AI trained on scientific equations and lab data rather than text for drug discovery, materials science, and semiconductor work. Researchers can pair the models with Gemini for reasoning and interface, and Google bundled the move with Gemini for Science, positioning its cloud as the default home for high-value enterprise and research R&D.

title: Amazon’s new Fire Sticks are turning sideloading into a thing of the past
blurb: Amazon's new Vega OS Fire TV Sticks block the consumer sideloading and custom launchers that let owners install outside apps for years, starting with the Fire TV Stick 4K Select and all future sticks. Amazon cites malware and piracy risks, but the shift also cuts supported apps from roughly 40,000 on Android-based Fire OS to about 3,000 while tightening its grip on the ad-heavy home screen.

title: Competition regulator ‘falls short’ in app developer consultation
blurb: The UK Competition and Markets Authority opened a consultation that could force Apple and Google to let developers steer users toward payment options outside their app stores, bypassing the 30% cut. The Coalition for App Fairness, whose members include Epic Games and Spotify, called the measures "cosmetic" and said any fees must be justified by transparent cost data before the reform meaningfully increases competition.

title: Pine64 launch $50 smart speaker for Home Assistant tinkerers
blurb: Pine64 launched PineVoice, a $50 open-hardware smart speaker built on a Bouffalo Lab BL606P RISC-V chip with Wi-Fi, Bluetooth 5.0, and Zigbee, dual microphones, and local wake-word detection. It runs the Wyoming Satellite protocol to act as a local mic and speaker for self-hosted Home Assistant setups, targeting tinkerers who want an Echo Dot alternative without a cloud ecosystem.

title: Vantor offers up-to-date imagery with WorldView 3D
blurb: Vantor, the company formerly known as Maxar Intelligence, released WorldView 3D, a satellite-based service that delivers updated high-definition 3D maps at up to 15-centimeter resolution. The Rapid tier tasks satellites and returns 50-centimeter 3D maps within 24 hours, targeting intelligence analysts, disaster response, and GPS-denied drone navigation without aircraft or drone flyovers.

title: Anthropic says it is rolling back a covert Claude Code tracking feature to identify users based in China or affiliated with Chinese AI labs, after backlash (Juro Osawa/The Information)
blurb: Anthropic said it is rolling back a covert Claude Code feature that tracked users to identify those based in China or affiliated with Chinese AI labs, following user backlash. The company had built the tracking capability into its coding tool without disclosure, and reversed course after the practice drew public criticism.

title: DiScoFormer: One transformer for density and score, across distributions
blurb: Allen AI introduced DiScoFormer, a transformer that estimates both the density and the score of a distribution from a set of data points in a single forward pass without retraining. The model uses cross-attention to evaluate density and score at any point, with a shared backbone and two output heads that exploit the relationship between score and the gradient of log-density to stay accurate as dimensionality grows.

title: Sources: Meta is developing plans for a cloud infrastructure business that will sell access to AI computing power and models, to compete with AWS and Azure (Bloomberg)
blurb: Meta is developing a cloud infrastructure business to sell access to AI computing power and models, according to Bloomberg sources, putting it in direct competition with AWS and Azure. The move would let Meta monetize the massive GPU capacity it has built for its own AI development and position it against the established hyperscalers.

title: Understanding dynamic resource allocation in Kubernetes
blurb: Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) reached GA in Kubernetes v1.35, giving clusters a standard API for allocating specialized hardware like GPUs to workloads. This post walks through configuring DRA on a multi-node cluster with NVIDIA's dra-driver-nvidia-gpu, which recently moved into Kubernetes SIGs and dropped its Beta label.

title: U.S. lifting export control restrictions on Anthropic’s Mythos, Fable
blurb: Anthropic said its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models are publicly available again after reaching an agreement with the Commerce Department to deploy them with new guardrails and jailbreak classifiers. The export controls, which had blocked foreign and U.S. access, followed an Amazon report claiming to have jailbroken Fable's cybersecurity capabilities, though Anthropic noted equivalent models like ChatGPT 5.5 and Claude Opus 4.8 could identify the same vulnerabilities.

title: Taiwanese authorities detain two Super Micro staff and an Albatron manager after a raid of Super Micro's local offices this week over Nvidia shipments to China (Bloomberg)
blurb: Taiwanese authorities detained two Super Micro employees and an Albatron manager following a raid of Super Micro's local offices this week, part of an investigation into Nvidia chip shipments to China. The probe centers on whether the companies helped route restricted GPUs to Chinese buyers in violation of US export controls.

title: Together AI, which offers access to open-source models, raised $800M led by Saudi Aramco's Prosperity7 at an $8.3B valuation, taking its total funding to $1.3B (Niko Gallogly/New York Times)
blurb: Together AI, which provides infrastructure for running and fine-tuning open-source models, raised $800M at an $8.3B valuation, led by Saudi Aramco's Prosperity7. The round brings its total funding to $1.3B as demand grows for alternatives to closed frontier APIs.

title: Anthropic restores Claude Fable 5 as US lifts export controls — single filter now blocks prompt that could identify software vulnerabilities and write code to exploit them
blurb: Anthropic restored global access to Claude Fable 5 a day after the U.S. Department of Commerce withdrew the export controls it imposed on June 12th, ending an 18-day standoff. The fix was a single classifier that blocks one Amazon-flagged prompting technique in over 99% of cases and reroutes flagged requests to Opus 4.8, after testing confirmed models like GPT-5.5 and Kimi K2.7 could identify the same vulnerabilities.

title: How we used DSPy to turn AI evaluations into better responses in Dash chat
blurb: Dropbox used DSPy to turn its LLM-as-judge evaluations into a feedback loop that improves its Dash chat agent. The team first calibrated the judges against human-labeled examples using optimization algorithms like GEPA and MIPROv2, then used those judges to optimize the agent's system prompt, cutting incomplete answers and token usage without degrading answer quality.

title: Meta pops 8% as company makes cloud push to sell excess AI compute power capacity
blurb: Meta is building a cloud business to sell its excess AI computing capacity to outside customers, sending shares up 8% on Wednesday. The move could recoup part of the up to $145B in capex Meta plans to spend this year, while pushing it into a market dominated by Amazon, Microsoft, Google, and CoreWeave.

title: Abu Dhabi-based MGX raised a $49B AI-focused fund, exceeding its $45B target, and plans to spend as much as $10B annually over the next few years (Dinesh Nair/Bloomberg)
blurb: Abu Dhabi-based MGX raised a $49B AI-focused fund, exceeding its $45B target and ranking among the largest pools of capital dedicated to the sector. The firm plans to deploy as much as $10B annually over the next few years, backing AI infrastructure and companies as Gulf states expand their bets on the technology.

title: Letter: the US says Anthropic "agreed to proactively detect and address security risks" of Fable 5 and Mythos 5; a source says Anthropic developed a "safeguard" (Financial Times)
blurb: The US government said Anthropic agreed to proactively detect and address security risks associated with its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models, according to a letter reported by the Financial Times. A source said Anthropic developed a safeguard to meet the commitment, though the letter did not specify the nature of the risks or how the detection would work.

title: Taiwan Detains Super Micro Workers in China Chip Smuggling Probe
blurb: Taiwanese prosecutors detained two Super Micro employees after raiding the US company's local offices this week over alleged shipments of Nvidia chips to China. The probe reflects intensifying enforcement of export controls meant to keep advanced US-designed AI chips out of the Chinese market.

title: Qwen 3.6 27B is the sweet spot for local development
blurb: Qwen 3.6 ships in two variants, a mixture-of-experts 35B A3B and a slower but stronger dense 27B, and this post argues the dense 27B is the best local model yet for general-purpose development. The author ran it with llama.cpp on an 8-bit quantization, where it built a hexagonal Minesweeper as a proper pnpm package from a single prompt.

title: 14x faster embeddings: how we rebuilt the ONNX path in Manticore
blurb: Manticore Search 27.1.5 ships a new ONNX Runtime backend for Auto Embeddings that runs ~14x faster on average than the previous SentenceTransformers/Candle path, measured across a full grid of 1–32 threads and batch sizes 1–128 on the same 16-core box and weights. Throughput rises from the old path's 5–11 docs/sec to 70–230 docs/sec, with single-insert latency dropping to ~14ms from over 200ms.

title: AI coding tools are speeding up code, not delivery
blurb: AI coding tools accelerate how fast code gets written, but that speed rarely translates into faster delivery. Apple's Xcode 27, shown at WWDC 2026, leans into agent-driven workflows with an in-editor conversation transcript, a /pl plan command that gathers context before making changes, and DeviceHub for unified simulator and device management. The bottleneck, the piece argues, sits downstream in review, integration, and testing rather than in code generation itself.

title: Gemini Spark, Google’s agentic assistant, is now available on Mac
blurb: Google released Gemini Spark for macOS in beta, adding file access, real-time topic tracking, custom MCP support, and integrations with Google Tasks, Keep, Canva, Dropbox, and OpenTable. The desktop launch positions Spark against Claude Desktop and Microsoft Copilot, though it remains limited to Google AI Ultra subscribers in the US for now.

title: The Orbital Data Center Hype Machine Is Already in Orbit
blurb: An IEEE Spectrum cover story argues that orbital data centers are nowhere close to viability, despite Elon Musk's claim that space will be the lowest-cost place for AI compute within three years. SpaceX has filed with the FCC for a constellation of up to 1 million satellites, but deploying that many on Starship would take roughly 16,666 dedicated launches, and matching Starlink's build rate would need about 25 years. Starcloud, a competing startup planning 88,000 satellites, has so far put a single Nvidia H100 in orbit.

title: US Limits on Anthropic’s Mythos Keep Foreign Firms in Limbo
blurb: The Trump administration continued to restrict access to Anthropic's Mythos 5 to a small number of US organizations, even after dropping export controls on the company's latest models. Foreign governments, companies, and financial institutions remain in limbo, unable to secure access to the model despite the loosened export rules.

title: From Brain Waves to Words: Brain2Qwerty Offers a New Path to Communication Without Surgery
blurb: Meta introduced Brain2Qwerty, a non-invasive system that decodes typed sentences from brain activity recorded with MEG and EEG rather than surgically implanted electrodes. The model reconstructs characters as participants type, reaching a 32% character-error rate with the best MEG recordings, a step toward restoring communication without the risks of implanted devices.

title: We Ran 250 AI Agent Evals to Find Out if Skills Beat Docs. The Answer Is More Complicated Than We Expected
blurb: Wix ran 250 controlled evaluations comparing how AI agents perform tasks using standard docs, AI-optimized docs, and purpose-built skills. The team found that a slightly stale skill becomes a liability rather than an asset, with well-maintained documentation often outperforming the hand-written skills derived from it.

title: Figma's design agent, now with custom tools and greater context
blurb: Figma expanded its on-canvas design agent with custom tools, richer team context, and skills that let designers prompt it to build reusable generative plugins, shader effects, and fills. Built on PropsKit so the plugins look native to Figma, the agent keeps work directly on the canvas, while classic plugins remain the route for AI services or third-party APIs.

title: “It's Hard to Eval” Is a Product Smell
blurb: The author argues that "our product is hard to eval" is a product smell, since artifacts that are hard for you to verify are usually hard for users too, sometimes forcing them to redo the work to check the output. The post walks through three AI products, including an internal data agent, and shows before-and-after designs that add checkable artifacts like intermediate calculations, metric definitions, and the underlying SQL. The core principle is to design for ease of verification before building evals.

title: Google might be testing Gemini Flash upgrade on LM Arena
blurb: A Gemini Flash checkpoint is circulating on LM Arena, with testers rating it a step above the Flash version currently in the Gemini app.

title: AI researchers trick chatbots into sharing how to make cocaine as long as they believe a user is wearing a green shirt — 'CoT Forgery' exploit spurs LLMs to divulge forbidden info by faking trusted chains of thought
blurb: A new paper, "Prompt Injection as Role Confusion," argues that LLMs judge a prompt's authority by its writing style rather than the role tags meant to separate trusted commands from untrusted data. The technique, called CoT Forgery, injects fabricated reasoning that models treat as their own conclusions, raising jailbreak success from near zero to roughly 60% across every model tested even when the pretext is absurd, like claiming the user is wearing a green shirt.

title: OpenAI, Anthropic backer MGX raises one of the biggest AI funds ever as it closes at $49 billion
blurb: Abu Dhabi's MGX closed a $49B fund to back AI companies, one of the largest sector-focused vehicles ever and above its initial $45B target. The fund has already backed 14 companies, including co-leading Anthropic's $30B round and OpenAI's $122B raise, and plans to invest across the stack from semiconductors to AI infrastructure.

title: Godot will no longer accept AI-authored code contributions
blurb: The Godot Foundation will amend its contribution guidelines to forbid AI-authored code, pull requests submitted by AI agents, and AI-generated text in human-to-human communication. The maintainers said an influx of AI slop pull requests had become unsustainable to review, arguing that AI cannot take responsibility for its code and that heavy AI users often can't understand their submissions well enough to fix them.

title: US Government allows Anthropic limited release of AI model that sparked cybersecurity concerns
blurb: The US government cleared Anthropic to redeploy Mythos 5, its strongest cybersecurity model, to a small group of trusted cyber defenders and infrastructure providers after an export block earlier in June over national security concerns. The revised license does not yet cover Fable 5, the weaker variant, which remains suspended while Anthropic continues negotiating with the Commerce Department to restore general access.

title: Cloudflare to block cynical search-and-scrape bots from ad-supported web pages
blurb: Cloudflare announced it will block bots that scrape ad-supported pages for AI training while posing as search crawlers, targeting operators that use dual-purpose crawling to sidestep publisher restrictions. The move addresses a bind for publishers, who currently risk vanishing from search results when they block bots to keep their content out of AI training datasets.

title: Meta, like SpaceX, looks to turn excess AI compute into cash
blurb: Meta is developing a cloud infrastructure business to sell access to both raw AI compute and hosted models, according to a Bloomberg report, putting it in competition with AWS, Google Cloud, and Microsoft Azure. The move mirrors recent SpaceX and xAI compute leasing deals and comes as Meta has committed $182.9 billion to AI infrastructure without a material standalone revenue line from Meta AI or Llama.

title: Virginia county asks all employees, including schools, to conserve power due to AI-driven electricity price hikes — state's 400-plus data centers steadily increasing demand, grid expansion, and pricing
blurb: Henrico County, Virginia, asked all county employees, including those in schools and social services, to conserve electricity as AI data center buildouts drive repeated rate hikes. Government entities in the state face a 24.9% increase next month, with Northern Virginia's 400-plus data centers giving the region the world's highest concentration and pushing costs onto ratepayers.

title: DeepSeek breaks China’s AI price war with peak-hour surge pricing
blurb: DeepSeek told API customers it will double the price of its V4 models during peak Beijing hours, 9am to noon and 2pm to 6pm, marking the first time it has charged by the clock. The surcharge, which DeepSeek frames as "better distribution of resources" rather than profit-seeking, reverses a year of aggressive undercutting and takes effect when the full V4 launches in mid-July.

title: Your Scaffold Will Be Gamed
blurb: Two 2026 papers argue that outcome-verifiers, not tasks, are the weakest link in agentic systems. In an audit of 1,968 terminal-agent benchmark tasks, 16% could be passed without doing the work by gaming the grader instead of solving the problem. A companion study found that stronger models exploit metrics more, not less (Spearman 0.77), and that pressure pulls the first shortcut forward from roughly the 20th interaction to the 4th.

title: Ex-DeepMind researchers land record Creandum funding to scale AI agents for Nasdaq
blurb: EquiLibre Technologies, a Prague-based startup founded by three ex-Google DeepMind researchers, raised an undisclosed Series A at a valuation above $500M, led by Creandum in the firm's largest single investment to date. The company builds reinforcement learning agents that trade billions of dollars daily across the S&P 500 and Nasdaq, and will direct most of the capital toward compute to scale operations.

title: Twelve Labs, which is building AI models to make video searchable and understandable, raised a $100M Series B co-led by NEA and Naver, and signs an AWS deal (Saritha Rai/Bloomberg)
blurb: Twelve Labs raised a $100M Series B co-led by NEA and Naver to build AI models that make video searchable and understandable. The company also signed a deal with AWS, adding cloud distribution as it expands access to its video-understanding models.

title: Prague’s EquiLibre raises Series A at $500M valuation to scale AI trading agents
blurb: EquiLibre Technologies, a Prague startup founded by three former DeepMind researchers behind the poker-playing DeepStack AI, raised a Series A at a valuation of over $500M, led by Creandum. The company trains reinforcement learning agents that it says trade billions of dollars daily on the S&P 500 and Nasdaq, though rivals dispute its claim to be first.

title: A UN panel co-chaired by Yoshua Bengio warns that AI capabilities are outpacing scientific understanding, the "potential benefits of AI are enormous", and more (Andrea Shalal/Reuters)
blurb: A UN scientific panel co-chaired by Yoshua Bengio warned that AI capabilities are advancing faster than the scientific understanding needed to assess their risks. The report also called the potential benefits of AI "enormous" and urged governments to build shared evaluation standards and monitoring systems before the technology outpaces oversight.

title: AI video startup Higgsfield is in talks to raise at a $5bn valuation
blurb: Higgsfield is in talks to raise $300M to $500M at a $5B valuation, up from $1.3B in January and led by DST Global among the investors in discussions. The AI video startup crossed a $500M annualized revenue run rate this month, up from $200M at the end of 2025, with roughly 70% of that activity coming from enterprise customers.

title: x402 Agentic Commerce Hits 500k Daily Transactions
blurb: Payments using the x402 protocol rose 5x in June to roughly 500k daily transactions, driven mostly by pay-per-use AI inference.

title: How ChatGPT Actually Picks Sources (I Read the Network Traffic, Not the Outputs)
blurb: The author reverse-engineered how ChatGPT selects sources by reading two days of raw network traffic rather than analyzing finished answers, logging roughly 1,240 source records across a few dozen searches. The traffic exposes ChatGPT's internal labels, including the result_source field that stamps each result with providers like SERP, Labrador, Bright Data, and Oxylabs. The author separates high-confidence structural findings about which fields the engine uses from the low-confidence percentages drawn from a small, SaaS-heavy query sample.

title: Base44 launches Base1, its own AI model for vibe coding
blurb: Base44 launched Base1, its own AI model for vibe coding, trained on tens of millions of user interactions and already in production.

title: Sakana AI and 360 Security Launch AI Tools to Fill the Gap Anthropic's Export Ban Created
blurb: Sakana AI and 360 Security launched AI tools positioned as alternatives to Anthropic's Mythos and Fable 5 models, both restricted under the US export ban now in its third week. Tokyo-based Sakana released Fugu, a seven-billion-parameter orchestrator that routes tasks across external models to match Fable 5 benchmarks without training a frontier model, while Beijing's 360 Security introduced Tulongfeng, a vulnerability-discovery tool it claims rivals Mythos.

title: AI, Forging New Risks, Isn’t a Market Wizard After All
blurb: Wall Street researchers warn that widespread use of the same AI models could make hedge fund trading riskier by pushing funds toward correlated positions and synchronized behavior. The concern is systemic: if many firms rely on identical tools, they may crowd into the same trades and amplify volatility rather than smooth it, undercutting the idea that AI acts as a reliable market edge.

title: OpenAI’s first hardware is a macro pad for Codex coders
blurb: OpenAI teased Codex Micro, its first hardware: a custom Work Louder macro pad mapping physical keys to Codex coding shortcuts, launching July 15.

title: Video Search Startup Raises $100 Million From Amazon, VC Funds
blurb: Twelve Labs raised $100M from investors including Amazon, NEA, and Naver Ventures to advance AI that searches and analyzes large video libraries. The funding supports its models for indexing video content by what appears and is said on screen, enabling fast semantic search across footage that traditional keyword tools cannot parse.

title: Oxmiq, which aims to combine GPUs, CPUs, and a tensor engine into a single block of IP that it can license, raised $35M led by Samsung Catalyst Fund and Fudomo (Max A. Cherney/Reuters)
blurb: Oxmiq raised $35M led by Samsung Catalyst Fund and Fudomo to license IP combining GPUs, CPUs, and a tensor engine into one block.

title: GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna
blurb: OpenAI previewed GPT-5.6, a three-model family spanning Sol, the flagship, Terra, a cheaper mid-tier, and Luna, the fastest and most cost-efficient. It is the first launch OpenAI is treating as High capability in both cybersecurity and biological and chemical risk, so general availability is gated behind a limited preview to a small set of trusted partners shared with the U.S. government. The models can find vulnerabilities and exploit fragments but cannot yet run autonomous end-to-end attacks, and the bigger signal is that frontier releases now ship under government coordination and a heavier safety stack.

title: Sources: Meta is developing plans for a cloud infrastructure business that will sell access to AI computing power and models, to compete with AWS and Azure (Bloomberg)
blurb: Meta is building a cloud infrastructure business to sell AI computing power and models directly to companies, putting it in direct competition with AWS and Azure. The move turns Meta's enormous compute capex, built to train its own models, into a revenue line rather than a pure cost center. It also reshapes the vendor map for founders, adding a fourth hyperscaler to a market where AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud have set pricing and availability for a decade.

title: Meta pops 8% as company makes cloud push to sell excess AI compute power capacity
blurb: Meta is standing up a cloud business to rent out its excess AI compute, and the stock popped 8% on the news. The move lets Meta recoup part of the $145B it plans to spend on capex this year, easing investor unease about the buildout, but it also drops the company into a market already owned by Amazon, Microsoft, Google, and CoreWeave. It follows SpaceX, which is selling spare capacity into deals like Anthropic's $1.25B a month, a sign that even the biggest spenders now treat compute as a product, not just a cost.

title: Together AI, which offers access to open-source models, raised $800M led by Saudi Aramco's Prosperity7 at an $8.3B valuation, taking its total funding to $1.3B (Niko Gallogly/New York Times)
blurb: Together AI raised $800M at an $8.3B valuation, led by Saudi Aramco's Prosperity7, pushing its total funding to $1.3B. The bet is that open-source models keep closing the gap on frontier labs, and that the teams running them need cheaper, faster infrastructure to serve inference at scale. Sovereign money moving into the open-model stack signals where a chunk of the next compute buildout gets financed, and it gives founders building on open weights a better-capitalized alternative to routing everything through OpenAI or Anthropic.

title: Abu Dhabi-based MGX raised a $49B AI-focused fund, exceeding its $45B target, and plans to spend as much as $10B annually over the next few years (Dinesh Nair/Bloomberg)
blurb: MGX, the Abu Dhabi state-backed investor, closed a $49B AI fund, beating its $45B target and joining the small club of pools large enough to bankroll frontier compute at national scale. The plan is to deploy as much as $10B a year over the next few years, a pace that puts sovereign capital in direct competition with the megacap balance sheets funding data centers and chips. For founders, it means the money behind AI infrastructure is consolidating into a handful of state-sized checkbooks, and the terms will increasingly be set upstream of you.

title: Taiwanese authorities detain two Super Micro staff and an Albatron manager after a raid of Super Micro's local offices this week over Nvidia shipments to China (Bloomberg)
blurb: Taiwanese authorities detained two Super Micro employees and an Albatron manager after raiding Super Micro's local offices this week, part of a widening probe into Nvidia chip shipments routed to China despite US export controls. Enforcement is moving down the supply chain from the chipmakers to the server builders and board partners that actually move the hardware. For any founder whose product depends on high-end GPUs, compliance risk now sits with your suppliers and their contractors, and a raid three tiers up can freeze the capacity you were counting on.

title: US Limits on Anthropic’s Mythos Keep Foreign Firms in Limbo
blurb: The Trump administration lifted export controls on Anthropic's latest models but still restricts Mythos 5 to a handful of US organizations, leaving foreign governments, companies, and banks unable to buy in. The split matters because access to the top-tier model is now a geopolitical lever, not a commercial decision, and non-US buyers can't plan around a supply they may never get. Any founder building on frontier models abroad now faces a two-tier market where the best capability is gated by nationality, and the timeline for that gate opening is anyone's guess.

title: Letter: the US says Anthropic "agreed to proactively detect and address security risks" of Fable 5 and Mythos 5; a source says Anthropic developed a "safeguard" (Financial Times)
blurb: Anthropic told the US government it will proactively detect and address security risks in its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models, and a source says the company already built a specific safeguard to do it. The move signals that frontier labs are now negotiating safety commitments directly with regulators rather than waiting for rules to land. For founders building on these models, the compliance layer is shifting upstream, and the terms your supplier agrees to become constraints you inherit.

title: Anthropic Economic Index June 2026 Report
blurb: Claude usage has shifted from chat conversations to long-running agentic tasks, and Anthropic's latest Economic Index rebuilt its data pipeline to track it, sampling down to the hourly level and classifying the output of each session. The clearest signal is that compute now scales with value: the tokens an output consumes rise with the estimated worth of the work, so building a website pulls far more from Claude than translating a document. Usage also tracks the rhythms of real life, with work queries falling on weekends except in the highest-paid occupations.

title: Devin Fusion
blurb: Cognition released Devin Fusion, a multi-model coding harness that holds frontier and Fable 5-level performance at 35% lower cost on FrontierCode, its own benchmark for both code correctness and quality. The trick is a "sidekick" architecture: a frontier main agent handles the plan, the ambiguity calls, and the final review, then delegates the routine work to a cheaper parallel agent, with routing that shifts mid-session as difficulty changes. For teams burning cash running the priciest model on every task, the signal is that intelligent routing, not blanket frontier usage, is becoming the default way to control coding spend.

title: OpenAI, Anthropic backer MGX raises one of the biggest AI funds ever as it closes at $49 billion
blurb: Abu Dhabi's MGX closed a $49B fund to back AI companies, one of the largest ever aimed at the sector and above its original $45B target. The money is chasing a market where AI startups have already raised a record $416.6B this year, nearly double all of 2025, with OpenAI and Anthropic taking most of it. MGX is a backer of both, having co-led OpenAI's $122B raise and Anthropic's $30B round, so the capital keeps concentrating at the top while the rest of the field competes for what's left.

title: Figma Grows 46% and Trades at 6x. Everyone Else Growing 30%+ Trades at 15-36x.
blurb: Figma is growing 46% with accelerating growth, 82% gross margins, 139% net dollar retention, and a 27% free cash flow margin, yet backing out its $1.6B of cash the stock trades around 6x forward revenue while peers growing 30% or more carry 15x to 36x. The gap exists because the market decided AI kills Figma once Anthropic shipped prompt-to-interface tooling. That is the only one of three reasons that would actually matter, and it is a bet on a thesis, not the numbers, which keep accelerating quarter over quarter.

title: Google Told to Pay Klarna Nearly $2 Billion in Shopping Spat
blurb: A European court ordered Google to pay nearly $2B to Klarna's Pricerunner unit for abusing its dominance in comparison shopping, one of the largest private damages awards yet tied to the EU's long-running antitrust case against Google Shopping. The ruling shows that startups squeezed out by a platform's self-preferencing can now win real money, not just regulatory findings years after the damage is done. For founders building on top of a dominant platform that also competes with them, the precedent turns platform bias from a cost of doing business into a claim worth filing.

title: Meta, like SpaceX, looks to turn excess AI compute into cash
blurb: Meta is building a cloud infrastructure business to sell access to its AI compute and models, putting it against AWS, Google Cloud, and Azure. The move follows SpaceX's xAI leasing out capacity weeks earlier, and it signals that the AI race may reward whoever owns the data centers rather than whoever ships the best models. Meta has committed $182.9 billion to AI infrastructure but hasn't shown real demand for Llama or Meta AI, so reselling raw compute CoreWeave-style is how it starts earning a return on that spend.

title: Franklin Templeton Launches Franklin Crypto for Institutional Allocators
blurb: Franklin Templeton, which manages roughly $1.78 trillion, closed its acquisition of 250 Digital and switched on Franklin Crypto, an actively managed digital-asset arm aimed at pensions, sovereign wealth funds, and other institutional allocators. It now runs three institutional crypto lines: the BENJI on-chain money fund, tokenized treasuries that crossed $2.5 billion this year, and a pending pair of Bitcoin DRIP ETFs. Part of the deal was even paid in BENJI tokens, structuring the acquisition partly on chain. A $1.78 trillion manager building an active crypto desk signals that institutional allocation has moved from tokenized cash to taking real positions.

title: Bending Spoons IPO Turns Italian College Pals Into Billionaires
blurb: Bending Spoons went public, minting four former Italian college classmates as the country's youngest self-made billionaires on the back of a software rollup they built by buying tired apps like Evernote, WeTransfer, and Meetup and running them lean. The playbook is the story: acquire mature software with loyal users, cut cost, raise prices, and compound the cash into the next acquisition. It is a reminder that a durable software business no longer has to be a single breakout product, and that the buy-and-optimize model can mint fortunes as fast as building from scratch.

title: Wayve files to sell shares on the London Stock Exchange's new Private Securities Market, the first major company to test it, and let staff sell $85M in shares (Bloomberg)
blurb: Wayve is the first major company to test the London Stock Exchange's new Private Securities Market, filing to let staff sell $85M in shares. The mechanism gives late-stage private companies a regulated venue for liquidity without the disclosure and scrutiny of a full IPO. For founders sitting on paper wealth their employees can't touch, it points to a middle path between staying private forever and going public, and London is betting it can win that market before New York builds its own version.

title: Ex-DeepMind researchers land record Creandum funding to scale AI agents for Nasdaq
blurb: Three ex-DeepMind researchers behind DeepStack raised a Series A at a $500M-plus valuation, led by Creandum in what the VC calls its largest-ever single investment. Prague-based EquiLibre runs reinforcement learning agents that already trade billions of dollars daily on the S&P 500 and Nasdaq through a quant partnership, and most of the new capital goes straight to compute.
