blurb: Standard Chartered initiated coverage of Morpho on Wednesday with an end-2030 price target of $60 for the MORPHO token, a trajectory it says would outperform both bitcoin and ether. The bank's bull case rests on Morpho Vaults pulling in TradFi capital through custody integrations with Fireblocks, Anchorage, and Taurus, positioning the second-largest DeFi lending protocol to scale with a projected 37x increase in on-chain assets by 2030.

title: Why Poland is the only EU country where crypto firms can't get a MiCA license
blurb: Poland is the only EU country without a functioning MiCA licensing regime, after President Karol Nawrocki repeatedly vetoed the implementing law that would designate the KNF as competent authority. That leaves roughly 2,000 registered virtual asset service providers in limbo as MiCA takes full effect, pushing Polish firms to seek licenses in Lithuania, Latvia, or Germany and passport back home.

title: Ethereum gets a new nonprofit focused on institutional adoption
blurb: Ethereum Institutional, a new independent nonprofit led by former Ethereum Foundation enterprise head David Walsh, launched to give banks and asset managers a neutral point of contact as they evaluate the chain for tokenization, stablecoins, and onchain financial infrastructure. The launch reflects the Ethereum Foundation narrowing its remit to core protocol stewardship, with separate groups like EthLabs now absorbing institutional outreach and ecosystem development.

title: Aave logs biggest network-growth day in nearly 5 years as DeFi interest returns
blurb: Aave added 1,806 new Ethereum wallets on June 30, its strongest single-day network growth since October 2021, even as the broader crypto market pulled back and total value locked held near $12.2B. The surge, driven by anticipation of the V4 upgrade and a revenue-routing mechanism called Smart Value Recapture, has revived DeFi interest, though analysts caution that new addresses must convert into real lending activity to sustain the roughly 9% weekly AAVE gain.

title: Ex-DeepMind researchers land record Creandum funding to scale AI agents for Nasdaq
blurb: EquiLibre Technologies, a Prague-based startup founded by three ex-DeepMind researchers, closed a Series A led by Creandum at a valuation above $500M, the firm's largest single investment. The company builds reinforcement learning agents that trade billions of dollars daily across the S&P 500 and Nasdaq via a quant-firm partnership, and will spend most of the raise on compute to scale the operation.

title: Wayve Files for Share Sale on LSE’s New Private Market
blurb: Wayve filed to sell shares on the London Stock Exchange's new Private Securities Market, becoming the first major company to test the venue built for private-company liquidity without a full IPO. The move gives the LSE a proof point for its bid to keep high-growth firms trading in London, and offers autonomous-driving startups a template for raising capital while staying private.

title: Nearly 1,700 UK investors sue Binance, founder CZ over alleged unauthorized derivatives sales
blurb: Nearly 1,700 UK investors sued Binance, founder Changpeng Zhao, and Abu Dhabi's Nest Exchange in London's High Court on June 29, alleging the exchange sold leveraged tokens, futures, and margin products to retail traders since 2019 without FCA authorization, breaching the Financial Services and Markets Act. KP Law says the group is pursuing more than £150M ($200M), and the suit names CZ as an accessory two years after Binance's $4.3B US money-laundering settlement.

title: Kevin Warsh to Speak at Sintra, Trump's $1.4B in Crypto Earnings | The Opening Trade 7/1/2026
blurb: President Trump disclosed at least $1.4B in 2025 earnings from crypto and memecoin ventures in his latest annual financial filing, while new Fed chief Kevin Warsh makes his first foreign appearance at the ECB's Sintra symposium alongside Lagarde and Bailey. The disclosure sharpens the conflict-of-interest questions facing crypto policy under an administration with direct financial exposure to the assets the CFTC and SEC are actively rewriting rules for.

title: AI’s Trillion-Dollar Debt Binge Fuels Century-Old Private Market
blurb: Tech borrowers are tapping the century-old private placement bond market to fund the AI buildout, selling debt directly to insurance companies hungry for long-dated yield. The shift routes trillion-dollar AI infrastructure financing through a channel that sidesteps public markets and bank syndication, giving insurers a growing stake in the sector's capital stack and its risk.

title: Europe’s Crypto Reset Is Underway as Venga Joins the First Wave of MiCA-Approved Firms
blurb: MiCA replaced the EU's patchwork of national crypto rules with a single licensing regime, and only 244 of the 3,000-plus previously registered firms had secured authorization by May 2026, with Barcelona-based Venga among the first to earn CASP status from Spain's CNMV. Approval unlocks passporting across all 27 member states, while holdouts including Binance are winding down regulated EU services and pursuing new licensing routes.

title: Citi slashes 12-month bitcoin, ether targets as ETF flows dry up
blurb: Citi cut its 12-month base-case targets for bitcoin to $82,000 from $112,000 and ether to $2,240 from $3,175, now assuming zero net U.S. spot-ETF inflows over the next year. The bank cited stalled digital asset market structure legislation, weak demand, and treasury-selling fears, after the ETFs posted a record $4B in net outflows in June.

title: AI, Forging New Risks, Isn’t a Market Wizard After All
blurb: Wall Street researchers warn that widely adopted AI trading models could make hedge fund strategies riskier rather than smarter, because when firms deploy the same tools they converge on correlated trades. That crowding amplifies volatility and raises the odds of synchronized selloffs, echoing the herding dynamics behind past quant blowups. The studies push back on the idea that AI is a market-beating edge, framing it instead as a source of systemic fragility regulators have yet to price in.

title: Qonto and Pennylane: French fintech friends and foes
blurb: Qonto and Pennylane, two Paris-based fintechs backed by Sequoia and Tiger Global, have become both partners and rivals in Europe's SME market, each integrating the other's services while expanding into all-in-one banking and accounting suites. Qonto, valued at $5B and now serving 600,000 SME and freelance customers, is awaiting a French banking licence that would let it offer lending directly rather than through its current Payment Institution status.
